title: Branches as movable refs

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Branches as movable refs; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Git Reference Documentation; current stable accessed 2026-07; section applicable to Branches as movable refs
sources.claim: A branch is a movable reference to a commit, while HEAD identifies the currently checked-out reference or a specific commit in detached state.

sources.locator: Pro Git; second edition online; section applicable to Branches as movable refs
sources.claim: A branch is a movable reference to a commit, while HEAD identifies the currently checked-out reference or a specific commit in detached state.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A branch is a movable reference to a commit, while HEAD identifies the currently checked-out reference or a specific commit in detached state.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Committing on feature moves the feature reference to the new commit while main continues to point at its previous commit. Your goal is to draw and manipulate a small branch graph while predicting HEAD movement. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Commits and history; Log, show and graph. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: A branch is a movable reference to a commit, while HEAD identifies the currently checked-out reference or a specific commit in detached state. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Remote-tracking references are local observations, branch names are not commits, and deleting a name does not immediately delete reachable commit objects. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: A branch contains its own copied files and commits rather than naming one position in a shared commit graph.

includes: Relate branch names, HEAD and commits without treating branches as copied folders.
Draw and manipulate a small branch graph while predicting HEAD movement.
excludes: Remote-tracking references are local observations, branch names are not commits, and deleting a name does not immediately delete reachable commit objects.

outcomes: Draw and manipulate a small branch graph while predicting HEAD movement.

examples.title: Branches as movable refs: direct case
examples.situation: Committing on feature moves the feature reference to the new commit while main continues to point at its previous commit.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: Committing on feature moves the feature reference to the new commit while main continues to point at its previous commit.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Reason from references and commit ancestry: branch names move as commits are created, while snapshots belong to commit objects shared across the graph.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Reason from references and commit ancestry: branch names move as commits are created, while snapshots belong to commit objects shared across the graph.

examples.title: Branches as movable refs: boundary case
examples.situation: Checking out a release tag detaches HEAD, so a new commit exists without advancing a normal local branch unless one is created.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: Checking out a release tag detaches HEAD, so a new commit exists without advancing a normal local branch unless one is created.
Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Remote-tracking references are local observations, branch names are not commits, and deleting a name does not immediately delete reachable commit objects.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: A branch contains its own copied files and commits rather than naming one position in a shared commit graph.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Draw and manipulate a small branch graph while predicting HEAD movement.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: A branch contains its own copied files and commits rather than naming one position in a shared commit graph.
misconceptions.correction: Reason from references and commit ancestry: branch names move as commits are created, while snapshots belong to commit objects shared across the graph.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: A branch contains its own copied files and commits rather than naming one position in a shared commit graph.

analogy.example: A route map can model Branches as movable refs: movable signposts identify current destinations, routes diverge, and integration reconnects selected paths.
usefulFor: Visualizing references, divergence, ancestry, merge bases, and reconciled paths
limitations: Branches are lightweight references to commits, not copied roads or independent containers of files.
Remote-tracking references are local observations, branch names are not commits, and deleting a name does not immediately delete reachable commit objects.

recap.summary: You should now be able to draw and manipulate a small branch graph while predicting HEAD movement. Keep this boundary in view: Remote-tracking references are local observations, branch names are not commits, and deleting a name does not immediately delete reachable commit objects.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Branches as movable refs without using the optional analogy and include one observable consequence.
Why is this statement incorrect: A branch contains its own copied files and commits rather than naming one position in a shared commit graph.
Apply the model to this case from memory: Checking out a release tag detaches HEAD, so a new commit exists without advancing a normal local branch unless one is created.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “Short-lived feature branches”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
